23.text: Disaggregated serving keeps the transformer math the same; the real change is where prefill and decode work happen and how they stop blocking each other.

24.text: Same tiny names task, so the systems lesson is not hidden behind a change in data or model complexity.

25.text: This lab simulates a workload, so reproducibility matters both for the training run and for the request-arrival pattern we want to analyze.

26.text: Threading is the star here because the lab is really about systems architecture: separate workers let prefill and decode stop fighting over the same execution lane.

27.text: Latency is the whole point. We care about time-to-first-token and throughput because serving architecture is judged by user-visible delay, not by elegance.

28.text: The queue is the handoff point between the two phases, where a finished prefill becomes decode-ready state instead of blocking later requests.

29.text: Each request carries its own prompt, timing, and decode state, because serving is really about how many such requests can coexist without stepping on each other.

31.text: PyTorch runs the actual model so the serving simulation still feels like real inference, even though the central lesson is scheduling rather than modeling.

32.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear and Embedding that make up the transformer's layers.

33.text: Functional API providing softmax, cross-entropy, and ReLU without module overhead.

35.text: Fix seeds for reproducible workload generation.

41.text: Construct the dataset file path relative to this script's location so it works from any working directory.

42.text: If the names dataset file doesn't exist locally, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

48.text: Read the dataset file where each line contains a single name for training.

49.text: Randomize training order so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical patterns from the dataset.

52.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters across all names, mapping each to a deterministic token ID.

53.text: The BOS token marks the beginning and end of each name sequence during training and generation.

54.text: Total vocabulary size: all unique characters plus the special BOS/EOS token.

60.text: Small model config, enough to demonstrate the serving architecture.

61.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

62.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast for this demo.

63.text: Maximum sequence length of 16 tokens, matching the longest names in the dataset.

64.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the full embedding.

72.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, bringing activations to roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

76.text: Self-attention WITH KV cache support, the distinction between prefill and decode lives here.

84.text: Accepts an optional KV cache: None for prefill (process all tokens), populated for decode (only new token's K/V are computed).

85.text: T_new is the full prompt length during prefill, but just 1 during decode.

86.text: Project queries for the new tokens only.

87.text: Project to keys for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

88.text: Project to values for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

89.text: If we have cached K/V from previous steps, concatenate the new K/V onto them.

90.text: Concatenate new keys with cached keys, the cache grows by one token each decode step.

91.text: Concatenate new values with cached values, growing the history by one token per decode step.

92.text: Total sequence length including cached history.

93.text: Attention scores: new queries attend to ALL keys (cached + new).

94.text: Causal mask adapted for the asymmetric case: T_new query rows vs T_total key columns. During decode, this is just 1 row attending to the full history.

95.text: Mask and softmax in one line.

96.text: Weighted values, reshape to (B, T_new, C).

97.text: Return output AND the updated cache, the caller decides whether to keep it.

100.text: The standard feed-forward network: expand, activate, and contract back to embedding dimension.

103.text: Expand the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension through the first linear layer.

104.text: Contract the activated representation back to the embedding dimension through the second linear layer.

106.text: Apply ReLU activation between the two linear layers, introducing the needed nonlinearity.

110.text: Transformer block that threads KV caches through.

113.text: Normalize activations before the attention projections so Q/K/V computations see inputs with consistent scale.

114.text: The attention sublayer with optional KV cache: during prefill it processes all tokens, during decode just one new token.

115.text: RMSNorm before the MLP ensures the feed-forward weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

116.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention.

118.text: Block forward: attention with cache, MLP, return updated cache.

120.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, preserving the representation and enabling gradient flow.

121.text: Add the MLP output via a second residual connection, letting the feed-forward network only refine rather than replace.

122.text: Return both the updated hidden states and the new KV cache, so the next layer or decode step can continue from here.

125.text: Full GPT with explicit prefill and decode methods, the foundation for disaggregated serving.

128.text: Table mapping each token ID to a dense vector in the embedding space.

129.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

130.text: RMSNorm on the combined embeddings before the transformer, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

131.text: The stack of transformer blocks that form the core of the model's processing pipeline.

132.text: Final linear layer that projects hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

133.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

137.text: Initialize all weights from a small Gaussian distribution to prevent exploding activations at start.

140.text: Forward pass with KV cache support. start_pos offsets the position embeddings, during decode, positions continue from where prefill left off.

142.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos, during decode step 5, this produces position embedding for position 5, not 0.

143.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

145.text: Retrieve the cached KV state for this layer from the previous step (or None for the first pass during prefill).

146.text: Save this layer's updated KV cache so the next decode step can reuse the computed keys and values.

147.text: Return the predicted logits plus all updated layer caches, giving the caller everything needed to continue generation.

149.text: Prefill is the wide, heavy phase: process the whole prompt at once, saturate compute, and prepare the KV state that decode will later consume token by token.

150.text: Just a forward pass with no prior cache, all tokens processed together.

152.text: Decode is the opposite shape: one new token, lots of KV reads, very little arithmetic. That mismatch is exactly why colocating it with prefill wastes hardware.

153.text: Single token forward pass with position offset.

159.text: Run on CPU since the model is small enough that GPU overhead would not help.

160.text: Create the model, move it to the device, and display its parameter count for verification.

163.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as all labs.

164.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this small model to learn basic character-level name patterns from the dataset.

166.text: Standard next-token prediction training loop, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

167.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

169.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

170.text: Prepare the input tensor containing all tokens except the last one in the sequence.

171.text: Prepare the target tensor containing all tokens except the first, shifted by one position.

172.text: Forward pass, ignoring caches during training.

173.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the correct next tokens at each position.

174.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

175.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

176.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate over the course of training for smoother convergence toward the end.

177.text: Set the learning rate to a linearly decayed value based on how far along training we are.

178.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model weights one step toward lower loss.

182.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

183.text: Disable gradient tracking globally, everything from here is inference.

191.text: Timing utility: warms up with 5 runs, then times 50 runs for stable measurement.

192.text: Warmup runs, let caches and JIT settle.

193.text: Execute the function once during warmup to trigger any lazy initialization or JIT compilation.

194.text: Record the wall-clock start time so we can measure total execution duration.

195.text: Execute the target function 50 times to get a stable average, smoothing out per-run timing noise.

197.text: Compute the average wall-clock time per run by dividing total elapsed time by the number of runs.

200.text: Measure the compute profile difference between prefill and decode at various prompt lengths.

201.text: Generate a random sequence of token IDs to simulate a prompt of the specified length for benchmarking.

202.text: Decode up to 5 tokens after the prompt.

203.text: Time the prefill phase: ALL prompt tokens processed in one pass.

204.text: Run a prefill forward pass on the prompt and capture the resulting KV caches that decode will build upon.

206.text: Time the decode phase: generate tokens one at a time.

208.text: Each decode step processes one token.

209.text: One token forward pass with KV cache.

212.text: Measure how long the autoregressive decode phase takes, separate from the prefill measurement.

214.text: Prefill FLOPs scale as O(T^2), quadratic in prompt length. Decode FLOPs scale linearly per step but add up.

221.text: Test with increasing prompt lengths to show how prefill scales quadratically while decode stays relatively flat.

222.text: Run the profiling function at each prompt length and collect the timing results for the comparison table.

223.text: Print timing and FLOPs for this prompt length.

236.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more variety.

237.text: Simulated prefill cost: 0.5ms per prompt token. On real GPUs, this would be actual compute time.

238.text: Simulated decode cost: 0.3ms per step. Cheaper per-step than prefill because it's just one token.

242.text: A serving request: prompt, timing metadata, and generated tokens.

243.text: A numeric ID that uniquely identifies this request in the serving system.

245.text: When this request arrives in the system.

246.text: Upper limit on how many tokens to generate per request, bounding the decode phase for this simulation.

247.text: Time-to-first-token: the key latency metric for user experience.

249.text: List that collects the tokens generated for this request during the decode phase.

252.text: Create a realistic workload: mix of short prompts (fast prefill) and long prompts (slow prefill). The long prompts are the problem, they block everything in colocated serving.

255.text: Loop through the specified number of requests, creating each with a random prompt and staggered arrival time.

256.text: 30% chance of a long prompt (8-12 tokens), these are the "heavy" requests that cause head-of-line blocking.

257.text: Fill the prompt with random token IDs drawn from the vocabulary to simulate a realistic request payload.

258.text: Stagger arrival times by 0.3ms, simulating real request arrivals.

262.text: Simulate a prefill operation: run the actual model AND sleep to simulate GPU time.

263.text: Real forward pass to get logits and KV caches.

264.text: Sleep proportional to prompt length, longer prompts take longer to prefill.

268.text: Simulate a decode step: real model computation + simulated GPU time.

269.text: Real single-token forward pass with KV cache.

274.text: Sample one token from the model's output distribution.

275.text: Scale the logits by temperature, apply softmax, and sample one token. This is the actual character generation step.

279.text: Colocated serving: ONE worker does both prefill AND decode. Processes requests FIFO, a long prefill blocks ALL subsequent requests.

281.text: Handle one request at a time in FIFO order: prefill its prompt, then decode all tokens before moving to the next.

282.text: Wait until the request's arrival time.

283.text: Sleep if the request hasn't arrived yet.

285.text: Prefill the prompt, if this is a LONG prompt, all subsequent requests wait.

286.text: Record time-to-first-token, this is terrible for requests stuck behind long prefills.

287.text: Sample the first token and start decoding.

288.text: Decode loop: generate tokens one at a time.

289.text: Stop on BOS (end token) or context limit.

291.text: Append each newly generated token to this request's output list, building the name character by character.

292.text: One decode step with KV cache.

293.text: Sample next token and advance position.

300.text: Disaggregated serving: SEPARATE prefill and decode workers connected by a queue. Prefill doesn't block decode. The key insight: while one worker prefills a long prompt, the other worker continues decoding in-flight requests.

301.text: Thread-safe queue connects prefill worker to decode worker.

302.text: Results collection with lock for thread safety.

303.text: Signal when prefill is done; wall-clock baseline.

305.text: The prefill worker owns the wide prompt-processing phase, then hands finished KV state across the queue instead of hanging around to decode tokens itself.

307.text: Wait for each request's arrival time.

310.text: Prefill the prompt, runs independently of any ongoing decoding.

311.text: Record time-to-first-token, now only depends on THIS request's prefill, not previous requests.

312.text: Push the prefilled request state onto the thread-safe queue so the decode worker can pick it up without data races.

313.text: Hand off the prefilled state (KV caches, first token, position) to the decode worker.

314.text: Signal that all prefills are done.

316.text: The decode worker lives on the other side of that handoff, interleaving many in-flight requests while the prefill worker keeps chewing through new prompts.

317.text: Dictionary of requests currently in the decode phase, keyed by ID. The decode worker processes all of these each iteration.

318.text: Keep running until prefill is done and all decodes finish.

319.text: Poll the queue for any newly prefilled requests that the prefill worker has handed off for decoding.

320.text: Drain the queue, grab all available prefilled requests.

321.text: Unpack the handoff: request, KV caches, first sampled token, position.

322.text: Add to active set with an empty generated tokens list.

323.text: If nothing active and prefill not done, briefly wait.

324.text: Test whether the prefill worker has signaled completion, so the decode worker knows when no more requests are coming.

328.text: Process one decode step for each active request.

330.text: Evaluate stopping conditions for each active request: did it generate BOS (end token) or hit the maximum length?

331.text: Request finished: record timing and results.

332.text: Acquire the results lock before writing, since the main thread reads results after both workers finish.

335.text: Continue generating: append token, decode one step, sample next.

336.text: One decode step with KV cache.

338.text: Filter out finished requests so we only continue decoding the ones that still have tokens left to generate.

340.text: Spin up one thread for the prefill worker and one for the decode worker, which will run concurrently.

341.text: Start both workers, they run in parallel.

342.text: Launch both threads to begin parallel execution of the prefill and decode workers.

343.text: Block until both worker threads complete, ensuring all requests have finished processing before returning results.

344.text: Join the second worker thread. After this returns, all generation work is done and results are ready.

345.text: Sort results by request ID for clean comparison.

349.text: Show the workload configuration: how many requests, their prompt lengths, and arrival pattern for context.

350.text: Generate the test workload of requests with mixed prompt lengths and staggered arrival times.

352.text: Label long vs short prompts, long ones are the troublemakers.

355.text: Run the colocated strategy where a single worker handles both prefill and decode in series.

359.text: Run the disaggregated strategy where separate prefill and decode workers operate in parallel.

360.text: Same seed ensures same model outputs.

371.text: Compare each request between the two strategies.

372.text: Extract how many tokens were in this request's prompt for the comparison table.

373.text: TTFT speedup: how much faster was disaggregated for this request?

374.text: Decode generated tokens back to characters.

375.text: Decode the disaggregated-generated tokens back to characters for the side-by-side name comparison.

385.text: Average TTFT across all requests, the headline metric.

386.text: Compute the average time-to-first-token across all requests in the disaggregated strategy.

387.text: Print the TTFT comparison. Disaggregated should show significantly lower average TTFT, especially for requests that arrived after long prompts.

388.text: Total wall-clock time for all requests to complete.

389.text: Compute the total wall-clock time for the disaggregated strategy to complete all requests.

390.text: Show the total completion time for both strategies side by side to quantify the end-to-end throughput difference.
